critiques.context: Public landing/splash page. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Is the first impression striking enough to convert a PowerPoint user?

strengths: Highly striking, modern aesthetics that establish a premium feel.
Strong, authoritative messaging directly addressing user pain points with legacy tools.
Clear visual hierarchy and high contrast on the call-to-action (CTA).

issues.description: The copy block detailing PowerPoint's history is slightly dense for an introductory interstitial slide. Consider breaking this down into a more digestible bulleted list or shortening the emotional impact statement to speed up conversion.

critique.top_suggestion: Since this splash page establishes extreme visual polish, ensure that the moment the user enters the actual editing canvas (ribbon/panels) the transition is seamless and does not cause cognitive whiplash. The editor must feel as polished and fast as the landing page promised, or the brand promise will break.

critiques.context: Login form. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Should feel clean and premium, not generic SaaS.

strengths: High visual polish and modern aesthetic adherence.
Effective use of dark mode and neon accents creates 'premium' feel.

issues.description: The provided screenshot is an onboarding/modal, not the core slide editing canvas or login form required for layout assessment. This limits feedback on structural familiarity (ribbons, side panels).

While visually striking, the high contrast and deep dark background potentially degrade readability of long-form text blocks when viewing actual slide content, especially for users with visual fatigue.

The CTAs ('SKIP', 'CONTINUE') are functionally clear but lack distinct hierarchy within their container. They should be given more visual weight separation from the surrounding body copy to guide immediate user action.

critique.top_suggestion: To bridge the gap between modern aesthetics and PowerPoint familiarity, apply the neon/dark theme primarily to peripheral elements (side panels, property inspectors, selection handles) while maintaining high-contrast, clean white space for the central canvas content. This keeps the visual 'pop' without sacrificing traditional readability or layout predictability.

critiques.context: Signup/registration. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Is onboarding friction clear and minimal?

strengths: The dark ink background coupled with vivid typography establishes a distinct, premium 'modern design studio' identity.
The use of minimalist animation elements (the dots below the text) suggests advanced functionality and polished polish.
The overall visual hierarchy in the modal is clean, drawing focus to the primary message and CTAs.

issues.description: Since this is an onboarding/signup flow (not the core editor canvas), the main goal must be conversion. The current overlay content ('The last time presentations got an upgrade.') is highly narrative and distracting, potentially creating friction rather than minimizing it.

While the visual design is polished, the CTA buttons (SKIP and CONTINUE) are styled similarly in size and weight. If 'CONTINUE' is the primary goal, its contrast or styling needs to be elevated above 'SKIP' to guide the user's eye immediately upon reading.

The use of large blocks of descriptive text within a modal (the paragraph explaining PowerPoint replaced flipping) adds cognitive load. Onboarding copy should be punchy and directly benefit-oriented, not purely historical.

critique.top_suggestion: Prioritize the conversion goal over the narrative experience on this specific onboarding screen. Reduce the body copy to 1-2 highly impactful bullet points (e.g., 'AI-powered templates,' 'Real-time collaboration,' etc.) and ensure the primary CTA ('CONTINUE') uses a solid, high-contrast fill that screams action.

critiques.context: Password recovery. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Does it maintain the brand aesthetic even on a utility page?

strengths: Extremely clean and focused layout, minimizing cognitive load.
Clear, unambiguous Call to Action (CTA) that guides the user immediately.
High readability and professional typography.

issues.description: The design completely abandons the established brand aesthetic (dark ink/neon accents) in favor of a generic, bright utility page look. This creates a massive visual inconsistency, making the product feel less premium and breaking the promise that every part of the user experience is 'super-modern.'

While clean, the page lacks any signature brand flair or personality. It could belong to any generic SaaS service, failing to leverage its unique ‘design studio’ positioning even in a utility context.
The transition from the dark, high-contrast editor environment to this light, minimal modal is jarring and inconsistent for brand immersion.

critique.top_suggestion: Implement a mandatory visual theme consistency layer. Even on utility pages (login/password recovery), incorporate subtle elements of the core brand aesthetic—such as using a darker background card or neon accents around key inputs/buttons—to ensure that every point of interaction feels like it belongs within the high-polish, modern design studio world.

critiques.context: Main dashboard. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Does it feel like a modern design tool's home screen (Figma, Canva) while surfacing projects clearly?

strengths: The visual aesthetic is highly successful; the dark mode with neon/vivid accents feels extremely modern and premium.
Typography usage is clean, high-contrast, and professional.
The initial brand messaging ('Good evening, Vision.') sets an ambitious, sophisticated tone.

issues.description: The entire dashboard functionality is obscured by a mandatory promotional modal. A modern design studio must prioritize immediate access to the canvas or project listing over forced reading/marketing copy upon login.

While the visual styling is excellent, the current focus (the pop-up) prevents evaluation of core dashboard elements needed for familiarity, such as 'Recent Projects' list layouts or quick CTAs to start a new deck. The underlying purpose of this view is currently unclear.

The call-to-action (CTA) within the modal ('CONTINUE') is necessary but lacks visual hierarchy compared to the main background elements, making it feel slightly disconnected from the overall clean design language.

critique.top_suggestion: Remove the mandatory overlay/modal on dashboard load. Instead, surface three primary actions immediately and visibly: 'Start New Deck,' 'Continue Last Project,' and a clearly sectioned list of 'Recent Projects' that are instantly actionable without forcing the user through marketing copy first.

critiques.context: Projects listing. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Does the grid/list of presentations feel PowerPoint-file-manager-like?

strengths: High visual polish and modern aesthetic
Excellent use of negative space to draw focus
Typography is clean and highly legible

issues.description: The current view (splash screen/narrative) does not convey the functional structure required for PowerPoint familiarity. It feels more like a landing page, not an editor or file manager.

If this is meant to be a 'Projects Listing,' it currently lacks standard organizational cues (e.g., filtering chips, date metadata, explicit folder hierarchies) that users associate with productivity suites and cloud storage interfaces.
The primary CTAs ('SKIP', 'CONTINUE') are visually strong but lack the consistent branding or placement expected in a comprehensive editor tool flow.

critique.top_suggestion: Focus the immediate design effort on solving the project listing view. It needs clear structural elements (e.g., metadata cards, sorting options) that immediately tell an experienced user, 'Yes, this is where my files are,' while maintaining the neon/dark aesthetic.

critiques.context: User settings. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Settings pages should still have the brand polish.

strengths: Modern and visually cohesive dark mode aesthetic
Clear sectional hierarchy using bold headings
Excellent use of negative space to separate content blocks

issues.description: The numerical count indicators (e.g., the '2' for Workspaces) feel too raw or basic, disrupting the high-polish studio aesthetic that surrounds them.
Spacing consistency could be improved slightly between major sections to give a smoother visual flow and rhythm.

The contrast presentation of the two theme previews (Dark vs. Light) is functional but lacks an active 'Compare' or primary callout mechanism, making the choice feel equally weighted when one might be superior for specific use cases.

critique.top_suggestion: Refine all data display components (like counts and badges) to utilize the neon/vivid accent colors and rounded corners found elsewhere in the UI, ensuring consistency between functional elements and aesthetic design choices.

critiques.context: The main slide editor — THE core UI. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. CRITICAL: the canvas area and toolbar layout MUST mirror PowerPoint exactly (ribbon tabs top, slide strip left/bottom, properties panel right, white canvas center). Evaluate PowerPoint familiarity rigorously.

strengths: Successfully adopts a visually striking dark mode with vivid accents, achieving the 'modern studio' aesthetic.
Maintains clear structural segmentation (ribbon at top, canvas center, side panels for properties), which aligns with traditional presentation software flow.
The dedicated 'ASSISTANT' panel on the right offers modern AI functionality while remaining within a recognizable sidebar pattern.

issues.description: The navigation structure is cluttered. The presence of both explicit ribbon tabs (Home, Insert, Design) AND the left-side 'SLIDES' panel makes the primary control locus ambiguous. It feels like a mix between Google Slides and PPT without clearly prioritizing one system.

The properties/context panel is split across multiple areas (the right 'PROPERTIES' panel, the AI 'ASSISTANT' on the far right). This dispersal of control elements breaks the expectation that all core formatting and context-specific controls should be grouped in one predictable location.

The canvas itself is visually bland (just text placeholder), which diminishes the 'visually striking' promise. Even when empty, the area needs subtle design elements or guiding visual cues to encourage modern input rather than feeling like a blank white sheet within a dark environment.

critique.top_suggestion: Consolidate and clarify the control flow by standardizing the property panel. Instead of having separate 'PROPERTIES' and 'ASSISTANT', designate one primary, collapsible context sidebar on the right (or left) that handles *all* contextual controls (formatting, AI generation prompts, design assets), while keeping the main ribbon purely focused on major document actions (Insert Slide, Theme Apply).

critiques.context: Org settings — billing, SSO, audit tabs. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user.

strengths: The card-based layout for plans (Free, Enterprise, Pro) provides excellent visual segmentation and clarity.
The use of a consistent dark mode background elevates the perceived modernity and premium feel of the product.
The primary CTAs ('Download,' 'Upgrade') are highly visible and actionable against the darker background.

issues.description: Since this is not a slide editor, the critique must focus on general SaaS UX polish. The overall design feels more like a polished corporate settings panel (e.g., Stripe) rather than a 'visually popping' design studio, losing some of the intended 'wow' factor.
The transition between sections (Billing & Plans vs. Current Usage) could benefit from more visual separation or grouping to improve scanability and reduce cognitive load.

While the dark mode is strong, ensure that all minor elements (like dividing lines or secondary text labels) maintain sufficient contrast against the dark ink background to avoid them feeling lost or muddy.

critique.top_suggestion: For the billing section specifically, use a visual hierarchy element (like an emphasized container or subtle gradient shift) around the 'Current Usage' summary to immediately orient the user and distinguish their current status from the available upgrade paths.
